content.problemStatement:
Tracy is teaching Charlie maths via a game called NNN-Cube, which involves three sections involving NNN.

Tracy gives Charlie a number NNN, and Charlie makes a list of NNN-th powers of integers in increasing order 1N,2N,3N,,˙so on1^N, 2^N, 3^N, \dot, \text{so on}1N,2N,3N,,˙​so on. This teaches him exponentiation.

Then Charlie performs the following subtraction game NNN times: Take all pairs of consecutive numbers in the list and take their difference. These differences then form the new list for the next iteration of the game. Eg, if NNN was 6, the list proceeds as [1,64,729,4096...][1, 64, 729, 4096 ... ][1,64,729,4096...] to [63,685,3367...][63, 685, 3367 ...][63,685,3367...], and so on 555 more times.

After the subtraction game, Charlie has to correctly tell Tracy the NNN-th element of the list. This number is the value of the game.

After practice Charlie became an expert in the game. To challenge him more, Tracy will give two numbers MMM (where MMM is a prime) and RRR instead of just a single number NNN, and the game must start from MR−1M_R - 1MR​−1 instead of NNN. Since the value of the game can now become large, Charlie just have to tell the largest integer KKK such that MKM_KMK​ divides this number. Since even KKK can be large, output KKK modulo 1000000007 (109+710^9 + 7109+7).

###Input:


First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
Each testcase contains of a single line of input, two integers M,RM, RM,R.
###Output:
For each testcase, output in a single line answer given by Charlie to Tracy modulo 1000000007.

###Constraints


1≤T≤10001 \leq T \leq 10001≤T≤1000
2≤M≤1092 \leq M \leq 10^92≤M≤109
###Subtasks


30 points : 1≤R≤100001 \leq R \leq 100001≤R≤10000
70 points : 1≤R≤1091 \leq R \leq 10^91≤R≤109
MMM is a prime number

###Sample Input:
1
2 2

###Sample Output:
1

###EXPLANATION:
This list is :
[1,8,27,64,125..]−>[7,19,37,61..]−>[12,18,24..]−>[6,6,6..][1,8,27,64,125..] -> [7,19,37,61..] -> [12,18,24..] -> [6,6,6..][1,8,27,64,125..]−>[7,19,37,61..]−>[12,18,24..]−>[6,6,6..]. Number of times 2 divides 6 is 1. Hence the answer is 1.
